content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 22,
1994
Penny Allen
Allen
& Company
355
K Street, Ste.
K
Chula Vista,
CA 91911
Re:
Your Request for
Confirmation of Telephone
Advice
Our File No.
1—94—119
Dear Ms. Allen:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you regarding the conflict of interest disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act.
1
Since your request is one for general
information,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
Nothing in this
letter should be construed to comment on any conduct which has
already occurred in this matter.
In our telephone converation of March 16,
1993,
I stated
that the Act makes no provision for the assessment of late filing
penalties on amendments to previously filed statements or reports.
Section 81004.5 provides that:
Any report or statement filed pursuant to this
title may amended by the filer at any time.
mending an
incorrect or incomplete report or statement may be
considered as evidence of good faith.
Section 91013, which provides the filing officer with the
authority to assess daily penalties for the late filing of a
statement of economic interests, only applies to the failure to
timely file the “original statement” and not amendments to the
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs. Sections
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Allen,
Penny
Page
2
File No.
1—94—119
original statement.
(See Section 91013(a).)
Therefore,
a filing
officer cannot assess late penalties under Section 91013 for the
filing of an amendment to a statement of economic interests.
It is important to note,
however, that there is a difference
between an amendment to an already filed statement and a filing
which is more than a mere amendment to such a statement.
Generally,
an “amendment” is an improvement of a thing witiout
involving a change in its substance.
(See Black’s Law Dict.
(rev.
4th ed.
1968)
p.
106 col.
2.)
This must be distinguished from a
supplemental filing which in fact j the substance of the
statement.
For example, if an official files a statement of
economic interests and later files supplemental information adding
a forgotten source of income, this would be an amendment to the
statement.
However, if the same official files a statement of
economic interests containing cursory information merely to meet
the filing deadline and, after the deadline,
supplements the
statement with extensive financial information not in the original
filing, the supplemental filing would likely be more than a mere
amendment.
In that case the official would not have met the
filing deadline because the true statement of economic interests
in fact was not filed until after the deadline passed.
In that
case, the late penalties of Section 91013 could be applied by the
filing officer.
Finally,
even when a true amendment to a statement of
economic interests is filed after the filing deadline and Section
91013 does not apply,
the late filing official is still
potentially liable under the Act’s administrative penalty
provisions
(Section 83116)
and civil penalty provisions
(Section
91005.5).
Both of these provisions permit the Commission or other
applicable enforcement agency to seek fines in an administrative
or court hearing for failure to comply with provisions of the Act.
However,
as set forth in Section 81004.5 above,
the amendment of
an incorrect or incomplete statement may be considered by the
hearing officer or judge as evidence of the official’s good faith.
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Wayne P.
Imberi
Political Reform Consultant

sections.question_synthetic: Whether late filing penalties under Government Code Section 91013 can be assessed on amendments to previously filed statements of economic interests, and what constitutes a true amendment versus a supplemental filing.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Late filing penalties under Section 91013 cannot be assessed for amendments to statements of economic interests, as that statute only applies to failure to timely file the original statement. However, a distinction exists between true amendments (improvements without substantive change) and supplemental filings that materially alter the statement's substance, with the latter potentially subject to penalties. Late-filed amendments may still be subject to administrative or civil penalties under Sections 83116 and 91005.5, though evidence of good faith in amending may be considered.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter confirming prior telephone conversation from March 16, 1993. Document uses implicit Q/C structure through telephone advice confirmation rather than formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections. Single typo noted ('witiout' for 'without'). No formal immunity provided per footnote 2.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether late filing penalties under Government Code Section 91013 can be assessed on amendments to previously filed statements of economic interests, and what constitutes a true amendment versus a supplemental filing.

CONCLUSION: Late filing penalties under Section 91013 cannot be assessed for amendments to statements of economic interests, as that statute only applies to failure to timely file the original statement. However, a distinction exists between true amendments (improvements without substantive change) and supplemental filings that materially alter the statement's substance, with the latter potentially subject to penalties. Late-filed amendments may still be subject to administrative or civil penalties under Sections 83116 and 91005.5, though evidence of good faith in amending may be considered.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms prior telephone advice that late filing penalties under Section 91013 do not apply to amendments of previously filed statements of economic interests, but distinguishes between true amendments and substantial supplemental filings, which may be subject to other penalty provisions.
